challenges.situation: Your boss called your deck 'brilliant' in front of the team. Now you notice yourself drifting toward flashy, visible work and away from the quiet work that actually matters.

options.text: Keep chasing the visible wins — the recognition proves you're on the right track.
options.feedback: You've let the applause become the scorecard. Steering by 'what gets noticed' instead of 'what's true' hands the controls to whoever grants the praise.

options.text: Write down what success looks like by your own standard, then check praise against it instead of letting it become the standard.
options.feedback: Defining 'good' before you're judged keeps your worth and direction anchored inside. Now praise informs you without governing you.

options.text: Decide praise is hollow and start refusing all of it as a point of pride.
options.feedback: Total imperviousness is just a different deafness. Praise carries real signal about what's landing — refuse it entirely and you lose genuine information.

challenges.situation: The work is never quite enough on its own. You finish something good but feel anxious and hungry, eyes scanning the room for a reaction that may or may not come.
challenges.question: Which force is driving this?

options.feedback: Healthy satisfaction is quiet and self-contained — 'I did good work, I know it.' The anxious scanning for a reaction is the opposite of that.

options.text: A need for external validation running your identity
options.feedback: When the doing isn't enough without the seeing, the hook is in. If worth was never secured from the inside, praise isn't a bonus — it's oxygen, and you'll do almost anything to get it.
Curiosity can wait and take it or leave it. The hungry, anxious quality here is craving, not curiosity.

challenges.front: Why is praise that comes only sometimes more addictive than praise that comes every time?
challenges.back: It's called intermittent reinforcement. Reliable rewards lose their power; unpredictable ones make the brain keep pulling the lever hoping this time is the hit. It's the same pattern that makes slot machines and social feeds compulsive — randomness, not generosity, is what builds the addiction.

challenges.context: A monkey is lured onto a crocodile's back by a feast thrown 'in his honor,' then told mid-river the real plan is to eat his heart.

scenarioA.label: Flattered by the praise
scenarioA.text: Glows at the honor, climbs aboard without question, and ends up helpless in deep water.

scenarioB.label: Awake to what the praise is for
scenarioB.text: Snaps alert mid-river, sees the flattery as bait, and talks his way back to the safety of the tree.

challenges.explanation: What saved the monkey wasn't avoiding praise — it was waking up to what the praise was actually for. As Epictetus warned, the moment your peace depends on others' approval, you've handed them the controls; anyone who can grant applause can also withhold it.

principle.text: Praise is a fine reward and a terrible master.
principle.subtext: When approval becomes the fuel your identity runs on, you stop steering by 'what's true' and start steering by 'what gets applause' — and whoever controls the applause now controls you.

reflection: Make a quick list of whose approval you find yourself working for. Now ask of your current work: would I still be proud of this if no one ever noticed it?
